summary: Gruber reports on Apple's unveiling of the iMac Pro, a machine starting at $4,999 that delivers genuine professional-grade performance with Xeon W CPUs, ECC RAM, and fast SSDs exclusively. He details how third-party developers demonstrated VR and creative applications that either run dramatically faster on the iMac Pro or can't run on any other Mac at all. The piece acknowledges Apple has been effectively absent from professional desktop hardware for years following the stagnant 2013 Mac Pro. While praising the iMac Pro as delivering on half of Apple's promise from their April media summit, Gruber raises two critical concerns: when the rethought Mac Pro will ship, and whether Apple will commit to keeping the iMac Pro updated annually as VR technology demands.
key_points: The iMac Pro uses an entirely different internal architecture from the regular iMac — it's not simply a cosmetic refresh with faster processors
Several VR applications demonstrated at the briefing can only run on the iMac Pro, making it Apple's first credible entry into VR-capable hardware
Prices escalate quickly from the $4,999 base to over $11,000 for high-end configurations with maxed RAM and storage
Apple's track record with the 2013 Mac Pro — impressive engineering left to languish — raises legitimate questions about whether the iMac Pro will receive regular updates
The iMac Pro delivers on half the promise Apple made in April 2017; the rethought Mac Pro remains forthcoming with no timeline
money_quotes: Apple is not fucking around with these machines, and neither are the people who will be buying them.
The Mac has gone from being a non-player in the burgeoning world of VR to a credible contender in one fell swoop.
Do not let the appearance fool you into thinking that the iMac Pro is just an iMac with a dark finish and speed-bumped processors. Internally, it's a completely different architecture.
But the 2013 Mac Pro was an impressive piece of engineering and design that Apple put a lot of effort into, too.
If I needed the performance of modern professional desktop hardware, I would order an iMac Pro today. I wouldn't wait.
The end game for VR is an experience equivalent to our real-world vision.
Apple has been effectively out of the professional desktop hardware game for a few years.
key_insight: The iMac Pro is genuinely impressive professional hardware, but Apple's history of neglecting the 2013 Mac Pro makes the real question not whether this machine is good today, but whether Apple will keep it current tomorrow.
